title: Anger at God for the Slow Disappearance
dek: When the divine presence you believed in retreats into silence, the body registers the abandonment before the mind can make sense of it. This is not a failure of faith, but a primal, biological response to a sacred contract broken.
bodyMarkdown:
# Anger at God for the Slow Disappearance

You wake in the deep, unrelenting dark of 3:17 a.m., the kind of night when the body smells of damp earth and the residue of yesterday’s sweat, a faint trace of rosemary balm still clinging stubbornly to the back of your hands. The air is thick, heavy like the slow press of wet wool over your skin, suffocating in its weight and stillness, pressing down on your chest and settling into the small hollows behind your collarbones. The muscles in your back—those small, stubborn levator scapulae that run from your neck to your shoulder blades—are clenched tight, as if bracing against an invisible weight that you cannot name but know all too intimately. You can almost feel the fibers of those muscles bunching and knotting beneath your skin, a physical barricade against the vulnerability that seeps in with the silence.

This is the hour your anger at God rises like a tide, slow and inexorable, swelling beneath the ribs in a deep, primal wellspring. It pulls the fascia around your diaphragm taut in a silent scream that the world refuses to hear, a tightening coil that squeezes your breath and leaves your voice trapped behind clenched teeth. The anger is a slow burn, like embers glowing under ash—quiet, persistent, and impossible to extinguish by sheer will.

This anger—the one born from the slow disappearance—does not shout. It does not break glass or slam doors. It seeps in, an invisible erosion beneath the surface of your days and nights, a body-first messenger that carries a grief so ancient it has settled in your bones and the fibers of your being. It curls in your chest like a cold, sharp stone, pressing against your sternum with a weight that makes your heart ache. It is not a failure of faith, nor a moral lapse; this is biology. The body knows the absence before the mind can make sense of it, and the jaw remembers the words not said, the prayers left unanswered.

## The Body Knows Before the Mind

You carry this anger in the masseter, that grinding muscle along the jawline, the one that clenches so fiercely when you bite down on your frustration. It feels like a steel trap closing over your teeth, relentless and unyielding, a physical manifestation of the words you cannot speak. The slow disappearance—whether it is the fading presence of a beloved, the erosion of a dream, or the quiet vanishing of a sense of safety—does not register immediately in your conscious thought. Instead, it settles in the tissues, thickening in the fascia that coats your shoulders, winding through the trapezius like a slow, silent knot of tension. You might notice the subtle twitching in your neck as these muscles resist relaxation, or the dull ache that creeps into your temples as the tension radiates upward.

Robert Augustus Masters, a teacher whose work I have returned to during long nights like this, reminds us that anger is not an enemy to be conquered but a guide to be followed. “Anger is the body's way of telling us that we are being violated,” he writes, and in the slow disappearance, what is violated is the sacred contract you had with the world, with God, with the very air you breathe. The slow disappearance is a violation so subtle, so drawn out, that it nearly escapes notice—except in the body, where it leaves a trail of tension and yearning. Masters speaks to the idea that anger is a primal signal, a biological alarm system designed to protect us and draw our attention to boundaries that have been crossed or promises broken. It is not something to suppress or be ashamed of but a vital thread in the fabric of our emotional lives.

You feel it in the early morning hours as your psoas contracts involuntarily, the primal muscle that holds your fight-or-flight readiness deep in the core. It tightens, pulling your lower spine forward, a small, stubborn protest against the creeping absence you cannot name. This tightening can feel like a coil wound so tightly it threatens to snap, a visceral constriction that makes your belly hard and immovable. The psoas is often called the “muscle of the soul” because of how deeply it connects physical tension with emotional states. This is not pathology, this is biology. Your body is telling you a story your mind cannot yet hold.

## The Anger of Abandonment and the Sacred Contract

There is a rawness to this anger, a brutal honesty that many women taught to hide their fury never allow themselves. The anger is not a neat or polite feeling; it is ragged and wild, a storm that rips through the calm façade you present to the world. You were told anger was ugly, that it would alienate, that it would push love away. Yet here you are, with the slow disappearance unraveling your sense of belonging, your tether to meaning, and the anger rising like smoke from a long-smoldering fire, thick and suffocating.

Thich Nhat Hanh, the Vietnamese Zen master who taught so many the art of mindful presence, invites us to meet our suffering with tenderness. He says, “When you love someone, the best thing you can offer is your presence.” But what if the presence you once relied on has vanished? What if God, or the divine presence you believed in, has retreated into silence? Your anger at God is not a betrayal of your faith—it is the raw expression of your body’s refusal to accept abandonment silently. This anger is a sacred outcry, a boundary set against the emptiness that threatens to swallow you whole.

The slow disappearance can feel like a betrayal so profound it shakes the very foundation of your trust. It lives in the trapezius and the levator scapulae, those muscles that rise and squeeze around your neck when you realize you are left alone in the unravelling. You might feel a sharp, burning sensation just behind your ears or a tight band closing across the base of your skull. It is a betrayal that the body registers with a sharpness that no theology can soothe. This betrayal is not abstract—it is raw and palpable, a physical wound that aches even when your mind tries to distract itself.

## Herbs as Medicine for the Body’s Anger

You do not need to suffer in silence, nor do you need to push this anger down until it calcifies into resentment or despair. The herbal world offers medicines that speak directly to the body’s language of rage and loss, engaging the nervous system, muscles, and subtle energetic fields in healing dialogue. In the tightness of the masseter and the diaphragm, herbs like skullcap and passionflower act as gentle relaxants, easing the nervous system’s overdrive without dulling your awareness. Their effects are delicate yet profound, like a soft hand uncoiling the tightened threads of your body’s response.

I find myself reaching often for a blend that includes holy basil (Ocimum sanctum), a herb revered in traditional medicine for its ability to restore balance during times of emotional upheaval. Holy basil is not a sedative—it is a medicine that supports resilience, helping the psoas muscle—your primal fight-or-flight core—to release without shutting down. When I take it, I notice a subtle loosening deep in my belly, a softening that feels like a breath finally allowed to expand fully after being held hostage by tension.

When the anger feels like a wildfire in the chest, a tincture of wild cherry bark can soothe the diaphragm’s spasms, allowing the breath to return to its natural rhythm. This is not about silencing the anger but about making room for it to exist without consuming you. The wild cherry bark’s bittersweet essence seems to cradle the heart’s sharp edges, inviting a breath that is deep and restorative rather than shallow and frantic.

For those nights when the levator scapulae and trapezius knot so tightly you can almost taste the tension as a metallic tang on your tongue, a warm compress infused with chamomile flowers and lavender can soften the fascia, coaxing the muscles to relax. The medicine is in the heat, the scent, the gentle pressure against your skin—a somatic balm for a body that remembers too much. The steam rises in gentle curls, carrying both the floral scent and the unspoken sorrow, wrapping around your neck like a soft shawl. This simple ritual invites your body to let go, even if only for a few moments, of the clenched grief it has been carrying.

## An Embodied Practice: Meeting the Slow Disappearance

Place your hands gently on your lower ribs, just above your waistline. Feel the rise and fall of your breath there, the subtle movement of the psoas deep beneath your palms. As you inhale, imagine the breath expanding into the tight places inside you—the masseter, the trapezius, the levator scapulae. As you exhale, allow the breath to carry away the tension, the knot, the silent scream. Feel the slow unfolding of the muscles under your hands, the gradual softening of the hardness that has taken residence in your body.

If the body tightens—the jaw clenches, the shoulders rise—do not resist. Instead, invite the muscles to speak their truth. Say to yourself, “I hear you. I see you.” This practice, inspired by the somatic teachings of Resmaa Menakem, honors the body as the first and truest witness to your anger. Menakem teaches that trauma and pain are held in the body, and that healing begins with somatic awareness—feeling the sensations, the contraction, the release. It is in this witnessing without judgment that the body can begin to untangle the knots of anger and loss, slowly reclaiming its natural ease.

Even as the slow disappearance fades into the background of your days, this practice roots you in the present moment, where anger is not an enemy but a messenger, a guide through the wilderness of loss. The physical act of placing your hands and breathing into the tightness creates a bridge between the mind’s understanding and the body’s deep wisdom, fostering a dialogue that can lead to healing.

## The Slow Disappearance and the Quiet Rage of Waiting

Waiting is a peculiar torment. It is a slow hunger that starves you in plain sight, a liminal space where time stretches and contracts without mercy. The slow disappearance often happens in the waiting—waiting for a recovery that never comes, waiting for a presence to return, waiting for a sign that God has not abandoned you after all. This waiting is not passive; it is an active, bodily experience of tension and longing that can feel like being suspended between worlds.

This waiting lives in the diaphragm’s tightening, in the shallow, clipped breaths that fill the lungs but never satisfy. Each inhale feels truncated, as if your chest is a cage that refuses to open fully. The breath becomes a series of quick, shallow gasps, never quite reaching the depths needed to soothe the heart’s unrest. It lives in the subtle stiffness of the cervical spine, where the levator scapulae hold your head in a rigid nodding of reluctant acceptance, a physical bow to the unbearable truth of absence. The body smells of stale breath and cold sweat, the scent of frustration mixed with fatigue, the raw aroma of sleepless nights and unresolved grief.

Gabor Maté’s work on trauma and addiction reminds us that waiting in pain is a form of suffering that often leads to disconnection—from self, from others, from the body itself. Yet your anger, your fury at the slow disappearance, is a tether back to yourself. The anger refuses to let the disappearance become a void. It insists on presence, on recognition, on the raw truth that something precious has been lost. This insistence is a lifeline, a fierce declaration that you are still here, still breathing, still fighting.

## The Jaw Remembers What the Heart Cannot Speak

The jaw remembers. It clenches in the boardroom, tightens during the long drive home, clenches again as you try to fall asleep. The masseter muscle, like a clenched fist, holds the unspoken words, the prayers that were never answered, the rage that you were told to hide away. You might notice the subtle grinding sound in the silence, or the dull ache radiating along the edges of your skull. This tension can feel like a locked vault within your face, a place where your deepest frustrations and fears are stored away, inaccessible yet ever-present.

Pema Chödrön, whose teachings on embracing difficult emotions have been a lifeline for many, says that the path of awakening is not about denying anger but about learning to be with it without being consumed. “The most fundamental aggression to ourselves, the most fundamental harm we can do to ourselves, is to remain ignorant by not having the courage and the respect to look at ourselves honestly and gently,” she writes. This courageous gentleness is the practice of meeting your anger not as a foe but as a teacher, allowing the jaw to unclench just enough to begin to listen.

This is the work—the unflinching, gentle gaze at the anger that rises when God seems absent, when the slow disappearance has left you hollowed out but still breathing. The jaw clenches because it remembers; the body holds the story long after the mind has tried to forget. The tension in your face is a monument to survival, a living archive of your pain and resistance.

## The Quiet Revolution of Claiming Your Anger

Claiming your anger at God for the slow disappearance is not a rejection but a revolution—a quiet, fierce reclaiming of your sovereignty over your own body and spirit. It is saying, “I will not be silent. I will not be small. I will honor the biology of my fury.” This revolution is embodied, rooted in the physical experience of anger as a vital force that demands acknowledgment and respect.

This is a radical act for women who were taught their anger was ugly, dangerous, a sin. Yet the body knows better. The muscles in your neck, your back, your jaw—they know that anger is a vital force, a protector, a communicator. This is not weakness; this is strength embodied. It is the primal power of your body rising up to set boundaries and assert presence in a world that may try to erase or diminish you.

In the long, slow hours of the night, when the world feels empty and God’s silence is a vast, echoing chamber, your anger is the flame that refuses to go out. It is the pulse beneath the disappearance, the pulse that says you are not lost, not forgotten. This flame warms the cold spaces within you, casting light into the dark corners where grief and rage have taken root. It is a quiet revolution, a defiant act of love toward yourself and your own survival.

---

## FAQ

**Q: Is it wrong to feel anger at God, especially when dealing with loss or disappearance?**  
**A:** No. Feeling anger at God, or at the divine presence you believed in, is a natural, human response to abandonment and loss. This anger is your body’s way of signaling a violation of trust and safety. It is not a moral failure but a vital message that demands your attention and care. It arises from the deep biology of attachment and expectation, a primal cry that your sacred contract has been broken.

**Q: How can I work with my anger without it consuming me?**  
**A:** The key is somatic awareness—feeling where the anger lives in your body and giving it space to exist without judgment or resistance. Practices inspired by teachers like Resmaa Menakem and Pema Chödrön encourage gentle presence with your feelings, allowing anger to inform you rather than overwhelm you. This means tuning into the physical sensations—the tight jaw, the clenched fists, the tense shoulders—and breathing into them, inviting release instead of suppression.

**Q: What herbs can help with the physical tension anger causes?**  
**A:** Herbs like holy basil, skullcap, passionflower, and wild cherry bark support the nervous system and help ease muscular tension, especially in the diaphragm, jaw, and shoulders. Chamomile and lavender compresses can soothe tight fascia and encourage relaxation. These herbs work subtly but effectively, engaging the body’s own healing mechanisms. Always source herbs responsibly and consult with a professional if you have health concerns.

**Q: Why does the slow disappearance cause such deep physical tension?**  
**A:** The slow disappearance is a prolonged form of grief and violation that registers first in the body. Muscles like the masseter, trapezius, levator scapulae, and psoas respond to emotional pain with contraction and tightness—a survival mechanism that becomes chronic if unattended. This tension is the body’s way of guarding itself against further loss, a physical manifestation of unresolved trauma.

**Q: Can mindfulness help with anger at God or loss?**  
**A:** Yes. Mindfulness practices, especially those that focus on bodily sensations rather than thoughts alone, help you stay grounded in the present. Teachers like Thich Nhat Hanh emphasize presence as a way to meet suffering with compassion, transforming anger from a destructive force into a guide. Mindfulness invites you to witness your anger without judgment, allowing it to move through you rather than becoming stuck.

---

Your body is a sacred archive of your anger, your grief, your slow disappearance. It is not a betrayal to honor this truth. It is the first step toward reclaiming your power, your presence, and your peace. In listening deeply to your body’s wisdom, you begin a journey of healing that embraces both the rage and the tenderness of being human.
